IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—improving word vectors for morphologically rich languages and long-tail word forms—by introducing morph-fitting that injects inexpensive, language-specific morphological constraints (Sect. 1, ATTRACT-REPEL). It demonstrates consistent intrinsic gains (SimLex / Morph-SimLex across EN, DE, IT, RU) and concrete downstream impact (DST joint accuracy increases, e.g. IT 74.1→78.1 and DE 60.6→66.3, setting new SOTA), showing this simple post-processing can change practitioner choices for vector specialization. The approach is portable (rules produced by non-native speakers) and complements existing lexical resources, so it is likely to be adopted and extended to other languages and tasks. While not fundamentally paradigm-shifting, it is a strong, broadly useful contribution that opens clear follow-up directions.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a clear overall organization (introduction of ATTRACT-REPEL, experimental setup, downstream DST task, related work and conclusions) and key notation for the model (e.g. BA/BR, δatt/δrpl, ReLU) is introduced. However, the provided text contains many presentation problems — repeated sentences and paragraphs (e.g. "Formalised as an instance..." repeated), LaTeX/artifact remnants and line-number clutter, and a garbled examples/constraints list and figure caption that make some parts hard to follow. Figures and equations are present and informative in places (Fig.2 and the cost terms), but the formatting inconsistencies and duplicated text substantially reduce readability.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — the paper sits squarely in top-tier NLP/ML scope: it addresses word representation learning and semantic specialisation using a concrete method (ATTRACT-REPEL extension) and language-specific morphological rules. It targets the right audience by combining intrinsic benchmarks (SimLex/Morph-SimLex) and a meaningful downstream NLP task (multilingual Dialogue State Tracking on WOZ 2.0 with the Neural Belief Tracker), and presents multilingual experiments (EN/DE/IT/RU). The problem formulation and evaluation protocol use community-standard methods and datasets and are positioned relative to prior retrofitting and morphology-to-embedding work, making it well suited for a top NLP/ML venue.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear main claims (morph-fitting using simple language-specific rules improves vector quality and downstream DST) and supports them with model equations (ATTRACT/REPEL cost terms) and multi-language experiments (e.g. IT 74.1→78.1, DE 60.6→66.3 joint accuracy) against reasonable baselines. The mathematical presentation is standard and correct for a post-hoc specialisation method, and the experimental setup uses multiple vector collections and intrinsic (SimLex / Morph-SimLex) and extrinsic (WOZ/DST) evaluations. Minor issues: the excerpt does not report statistical significance testing, and the reliance on very simple rule-based antonymy/infl ection constraints (created by non-native speakers) risks noisy or incomplete supervision that is not quantified in the provided text. These are caveats but do not undermine the main empirical findings.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to a wide range of appropriate baselines and prior work (e.g., PARAGRAM/ATTRACT-REPEL, Faruqui et al. retrofitting, Cotterell et al. (2016), morphology-aware and character/subword models, and many distributional vector collections such as SGNS, GloVe, BiSkip, MultiCCA) as described in the Related Work and experimental setup. Comparisons appear fair and consistent: the same pre-trained vector collections are used and evaluated intrinsically on SimLex/Morph-SimLex and extrinsically on DST (WOZ) with joint goal accuracy, and they even report trying an alternative post-processing model (Mrkšić et al., 2016). The paper acknowledges when gains are limited (e.g., English shows little variation and SimLex gains do not always translate to DST) and analyses differences (pipelined decoupling of morphology, inclusion of antonyms, discussion of morph-fixed vs morph-fitted effects), and it cites and discusses the most relevant competing approaches throughout.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper does not propose a wholly new retrofitting algorithm (it explicitly builds on the ATTRACT-REPEL/PARAGRAM family and situates itself as a post-processing semantic specialisation method), but it introduces a notable and practical novelty: generating ATTRACT/REPEL constraints from simple language-specific morphological rules rather than curated lexicons (paper: “morph-fitting is steered by a set of linguistic constraints derived from simple language-specific rules” and “In spirit, our work is similar to Cotterell et al. (2016) … Their work uses gold morphological lexicons; we show that competitive performance can be achieved using a non-exhaustive set of simple rules”). The paper clearly differentiates this contribution from prior work and demonstrates multilingual and downstream (DST) benefits, making it a meaningful, portable extension rather than a purely incremental re-use.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper presents comprehensive experiments: intrinsic evaluations on SimLex / Morph-SimLex (and SimVerb references) across four languages and an extrinsic DST downstream evaluation (WOZ 2.0) demonstrating gains and even new SOTA for DE/IT (Fig.2 and accompanying text). It includes sensible ablations/variants (distributional, morph-fixed, MFIT-A, MFIT-AR) and compares multiple vector collections and a different post-processing method, addressing sensitivity to the morph-fitting choices. Related work is discussed thoroughly (semantic specialisation, retrofitting, morphology-aware models) and several limitations are acknowledged (small English gains, discrepancy between intrinsic/extrinsic metrics, morph-fixed negative effect, and future work). Reproducibility is partially supported (objective functions, many SGNS and training settings, dataset sources), but some critical details (exact morphological-rule sets per language, specific margin/regulariser values) are not present in the provided text, leaving minor gaps for full replication.
